title: Ito-Kakimura-Kamiyama-Kobayashi-Okamoto Conjecture
statement_text: There exists a constant-factor approximation algorithm for the problem of finding a shortest path between two perfect matchings on the perfect matching polytope of a bipartite graph.
context_text: Ito, Kakimura, Kamiyama, Kobayashi, and Okamoto proved a 3/2-inapproximability result for shortest paths on perfect matching polytopes of bipartite graphs, yet conjectured that a constant-factor polynomial-time approximation algorithm still exists. This conjecture is the central target of the present paper, which disproves it.
attributed_to: Ito, Kakimura, Kamiyama, Kobayashi, and Okamoto
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture under P $\neq$ NP by showing that for every fixed integer $k \geq 2$ no polynomial-time algorithm can output a path of length at most $k$ between two vertices at distance 2 of the perfect matching polytope of a bipartite graph; under ETH this is strengthened to show no polynomial-time algorithm can achieve approximation ratio less than $\left(\frac{1}{4}-o(1)\right)\frac{\log N}{\log\log N}$ on $N$-vertex instances.
notes: The conjecture is stated in running prose in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment; the statement above is a direct transcription of the paper's own description of it.
paper_title: Inapproximability of shortest paths on perfect matching polytopes
